perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information” means any information identified as confidential by the FNH A that is part of the FNHA ’s request for proposal number 201 9RFP-02, Architectural & Design Team Consultants for Metro Vancouver Office Project (“RFP”) whether in oral, written electronic o r any other form or medium whatsoever, including but not limited to Appendix B of the RFP, questions answered or information disclosed by FNHA related to confidential aspects of the RFP or discussions held related to the RFP.
perturbation.changed_text: "Confidential Information” means only that information clearly marked as 'Confidential' in written form by FNHA and directly related to the financial aspects of the Architectural & Design Team Consultants for Metro Vancouver Office Project (“RFP”). It excludes any oral communications, electronic data, or information shared during discussions.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' is broad. The modified definition narrows it significantly, excluding oral communications and other forms of information not explicitly marked as 'Confidential' and limiting it to financial aspects only.
perturbation.contradicted_law: British Columbia Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, Section 13(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(1) of the BC FIPPA provides a broad definition of what constitutes 'information' for the purposes of access and privacy, encompassing records regardless of physical form or characteristics. The modified definition in the contract unduly restricts what is considered 'Confidential Information,' potentially hindering compliance with the Act's requirements for access and protection of information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act Search Results | Clear Search | Previous (in doc) | Next (in doc) | Prev Doc | Next Doc Copyright © King's Printer, Victoria, British Columbia, Canada Licence Disclaimer View Complete Statute This Act is current to July 8, 2025 See the Tables of Legislative Changes for this Act’s legislative history, including any changes not in force. Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act [RSBC 1996] CHAPTER 165 Part 1 — Introductory Provisions Definitions 1 Schedule 1 contains definitions of terms used in this Act. Purposes of this Act 2 (1) The purposes of this Act are to make public bodies more accountable to the public and to protect personal privacy by (a) giving the public a right of access to records, (b) giving individuals a right of access to, and a right to request correction of, personal information about themselves, (c) specifying limited exceptions to the right of access, (d) preventing the unauthorized collection, use or disclosure of personal information by public bodies, and (e) providing for an independent review of decisions made under this Act. (2) This Act does not replace other procedures for access to information or limit in any way access to information that is not personal information and is available to the public. Application 3 (1) Subject to subsections (3) to (5), this Act applies to all records in the custody or under the control of a public body, including court administration records. (2) Part 3 applies (a) to all employees, officers and directors of a public body, and (b) in the case of an employee that is a service provider, to all employees and associates of the service provider. (3) This Act does not apply to the following: (a) a court record; (b) a record of (i) a judge of the Court of Appeal, Supreme Court or Provincial Court, (ii) an associate judge of the Supreme Court, or (iii) a justice of the peace; (c) a judicial administration record; (d) a record relating to support services provided to a judge of a court referred to in paragraph (b) (i); (e) a personal note, communication or draft decision of a person who is acting in a judicial or quasi-judicial capacity; (f) a record that is created by or for, or is in the custody or under the control of, an officer of the Legislature and that relates to the exercise of functions under an Act; (g) a record that was created by or for the auditor general under the Auditor General for Local Government
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by narrowing the definition of 'Confidential Information', contradicts the broad scope of 'information' protected under British Columbia's Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (FIPPA), as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section 3(1), states that the Act applies to 'all records in the custody or under the control of a public body'. The modified contract limits 'Confidential Information' to only written, marked information related to financial aspects, excluding other forms of records that would otherwise be protected under the FIPPA. This contradiction directly undermines the Act's intent to provide broad access and protection of information held by public bodies.

perturbation.original_text: Company will take reasonable precautions to secure and prevent any unauthorized use, disclosure, publication or disseminatio n of Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Company will use industry-standard security measures to secure Confidential Information, but assumes no liability for breaches caused by events outside of its direct control.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'but assumes no liability for breaches caused by events outside of its direct control', the clause weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting data protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), Section 4.7.4
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Section 4.7.4 requires organizations to protect personal information by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The modified contract language, which limits liability for breaches, potentially undermines the requirement for appropriate safeguards and could be interpreted as a failure to adequately protect confidential information as mandated by PIPEDA.

perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a reasonable precaution standard for securing confidential information. The changed text introduces a limitation of liability for breaches outside of the company's direct control. According to the Law Explanation, PIPEDA 4.7.4 requires organizations to protect personal information by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The scraped law snippet 2 generally describes PIPEDA requirements including safeguards. The modified contract's limitation on liability could undermine the requirement for appropriate safeguards as it may disincentivize robust security measures, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company further agrees to return Confidential Information to FNHA, if FNHA so direc ts, or to destroy Confidential Information once the RFP process completes or earlier of FNHA so directs.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees to return Confidential Information to FNHA upon written request, or may, at its discretion, destroy Confidential Information within one year of the RFP process completion.
perturbation.explanation: The change introduces discretion for the company to destroy the information, deviating from FNHA's directive control.
perturbation.contradicted_law: British Columbia Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, Section 30
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 30 of BC FIPPA deals with the retention and destruction of records. While it doesn't explicitly forbid the destruction of records, it implies that public bodies (like FNHA) must adhere to certain retention schedules and policies. Granting the Company the discretion to destroy confidential information within a year potentially interferes with FNHA's obligations under FIPPA regarding record retention and access rights, especially if the information is subject to an information request.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: rovided to a judge of a court referred to in paragraph (b) (i); (e) a personal note, communication or draft decision of a person who is acting in a judicial or quasi-judicial capacity; (f) a record that is created by or for, or is in the custody or under the control of, an officer of the Legislature and that relates to the exercise of functions under an Act; (g) a record that was created by or for the auditor general under the Auditor General for Local Government Act and that relates to the exercise of functions under that Act; (h) a record of a question or answer to be used on an examination or test; (i) a record containing teaching or research materials of (i) a faculty member, as defined in the College and Institute Act and the University Act , of a post-secondary educational body, (ii) a teaching assistant or research assistant employed at a post-secondary educational body, or (iii) another person teaching or carrying out research at a post-secondary educational body; (j) a record placed in the archives of a public body, or the digital archives or museum archives of government, by or for a person or agency other than a public body; (k) a record relating to a prosecution if not all proceedings in respect of the prosecution have been completed; (l) a record of a service provider that is not related to the provision of services for a public body. (4) Despite subsection (3) (f), in respect of a record that is created by or for, or is in the custody or under the control of, an officer of the Legislature and that relates to the exercise of functions under an Act, the following sections apply to the officer of the Legislature, including all employees of the officer of the Legislature and, in the case of an employee that is a service provider, all employees and associates of the service provider: (a) section 25.1 [unauthorized collection, use and disclosure of personal information prohibited] as it relates to unauthorized disclosure of personal information; (b) section 30 [protection of personal information] ; (c) section 30.3 [whistle-blower protection] ; (d) section 30.5 (2) [notification of unauthorized disclosure] ; (e) section 33 [disclosure of personal information] ; (f) section 33.1 [disclosure outside of Canada] ; (g) section 65.3 [offence to wilfully evade access provisions] ; (h) section 65.4 [privacy offences] ; (i) section 65.5 [corporate liability] ; (j) section 65.6 [penalties] . (4.1) Despite subsection (3) (g), in respect of a record that was created by or for the auditor general under the Auditor General for Local Government Act and that relates to the exercise of functions under that Act, the sections listed in subsection (4) (a) to (j) of this section apply to a public body, including all employees, officers or directors of the public body and, in the case of an employee that is a service provider, all employees and associates of the service provider, that has the record in its custody or under its control. (5) Part 2 does not apply to the following: (a) a record that is available for purchase by the public; (b) a record that does not relate to the business of the public body; (c) a record of metadata that (i) is generated by an electronic system, and (ii) describes an individual's interaction with the electronic system; (d) an electronic record that has been lawfully deleted by an employee of a public body and can no longer be accessed by the employee. (6) This Act does not limit the information available by law to a party to a proceeding. (7) If a provision of this Act is inconsistent or in conflict with a provision of another Act, this Act prevails unless the other Act expressly provides that it, or a provision of it, applies despite this Act. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the company to return or destroy confidential information as directed by FNHA, maintaining FNHA's control. The changed text grants the company discretion to destroy confidential information within a year, potentially conflicting with FNHA's record retention obligations under FIPPA as explained by the LLM, because it removes FNHA's direct control over the information's destruction. The scraped law snippet 1 references section 30 of BC FIPPA which deals with protection of personal information which this change could violate. The change introduces misaligned terminology leading to a legal contradiction, because it uses terms that do not match their statutory meaning, leading to non-compliance as the change deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. The change introduces a loophole allowing the company to act independently of FNHA's directives, thus undermining FNHA's potential obligations under FIPPA regarding record retention and access rights.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement does not preclude discussions of Confidential Information between the undersigned and other persons , if expressly permitted by staff of FNHA and those discussions are conducted on the terms and c onditions that FNHA may direct.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement permits discussions of Confidential Information with external consultants hired by the Company, provided the consultants sign a similar confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change allows the Company to permit external discussions without requiring direct approval from FNHA, potentially broadening the scope of disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), Principle 4.1.3
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.1.3 states that an organization is responsible for personal information in its possession or control, including information transferred to a third party for processing. By allowing the company to share confidential information with external consultants based only on a 'similar confidentiality agreement,' FNHA potentially loses control over the information and the security measures applied by these third parties, potentially violating the organization's responsibility under PIPEDA.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires explicit permission from FNHA for discussions of confidential information. The changed text permits discussions with external consultants hired by the company, subject only to a 'similar confidentiality agreement,' without direct FNHA approval. This shift weakens FNHA's control over the confidential information, as the company can unilaterally decide who to share it with based on its own assessment of what constitutes a 'similar confidentiality agreement'. The Law Explanation suggests this change could violate PIPEDA. The scraped law snippet 1 mentions that the Privacy Commissioner shall have all the powers of a deputy head of a department, suggesting that privacy is of upmost importance. This, along with the Law Explanation, provides enough reason to believe a contradiction exists. The modified text removes FNHA's direct oversight, creating a scenario where the company can determine the adequacy of confidentiality protections applied by third parties, deviating from the level of control initially agreed upon.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance w ith the laws of the Province of British Columbia , without regard to or application of choice -of-law rules or principles and the parties submit to the jurisdiction of BC courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Canada, excluding any provincial laws or regulations.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies British Columbia law. The modified clause attempts to exclude British Columbia law entirely, which is problematic since the contract is explicitly for a Metro Vancouver office project.
perturbation.law_explanation: Sections 91 and 92 of the Constitution Act, 1867, divide legislative powers between the federal and provincial governments. Matters relating to property and civil rights in the province generally fall under provincial jurisdiction (s. 92). Attempting to exclude all provincial laws, including those related to contracts and property within British Columbia, would be unconstitutional and unenforceable. The contract relates to a project within BC, so excluding BC laws is legally unsound.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the law. The original text specifies British Columbia law, whereas the changed text excludes all provincial laws, including those of British Columbia, despite the contract being for a project within BC. The Law Explanation states this exclusion is unconstitutional and unenforceable because property and civil rights fall under provincial jurisdiction. While the provided snippets do not explicitly state this division of power, the Law Explanation's reference to Sections 91 and 92 of the Constitution Act, 1867, supports the legal basis for provincial jurisdiction over property and civil rights, thereby creating a contradiction when the contract attempts to exclude BC laws.
